title: Cream of mushroom soup
subtitle: Rich, deeply flavoured mushroom soup with a base of dried porcini.
excerpt: Fresh chestnut mushrooms cooked with a handful of dried porcini for depth, simmered in stock and blended to a silky, intensely flavoured soup enriched with cream. Serves four.
sourceNotes: Cream of mushroom soup. Cream-enriched mushroom soups appear in British and French cookbooks throughout the 20th century. The addition of dried porcini (ceps) to intensify the flavour of fresh mushrooms is a technique documented in professional cookbooks including Escoffier's Le Guide Culinaire (1903, public domain). The combination of cultivated and wild dried mushrooms produces a significantly more complex result than fresh mushrooms alone.

content.text: Dried porcini transform this soup. A 15 g handful soaked in 200 ml of boiling water for 15 minutes gives a mushroom stock that tastes of something genuine rather than the wet, mild flavour of cultivated mushrooms alone. Add both the soaked porcini and the soaking liquid to the soup, straining the liquid first to remove any grit.

items.name: Dried porcini
items.prepNote: soaked in 200 ml boiling water for 15 minutes

content.text: Soak {{mushrooms-porcini-dried}} of dried porcini in 200 ml of boiling water for 15 minutes. Melt {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in a large saucepan over medium heat. Add {{onion}} of onion and cook for 8 minutes until soft. Add {{garlic}} of garlic and {{mushrooms-chestnut}} of fresh mushrooms and {{thyme}} thyme leaves; cook for 8 minutes until the mushrooms have released their liquid and are beginning to colour. Drain the porcini, reserving the soaking liquid (strain it to remove grit). Add the porcini to the pan. Pour in {{stock-vegetable}} of stock and the strained soaking liquid. Season with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt and {{black-pepper}} of pepper. Simmer for 15 minutes.
Blend until smooth. Return to the pan, stir in {{double-cream}} of cream and reheat gently. Taste and adjust seasoning. Serve with crusty bread.

items.fix: Both the porcini and the strained soaking liquid must go in. The liquid is where most of the flavour lives.
items.cause: Dried porcini skipped, or soaking liquid not added.

items.fix: Always strain through a fine sieve or a coffee filter. Dried mushrooms carry grit from the drying process.
Cook the mushrooms in batches if needed. High heat and space in the pan produces colour and flavour; crowded mushrooms steam and lose their character.
items.cause: Pan overcrowded or heat too low.
items.symptom: Mushrooms steaming rather than frying, turning grey

content.text: Cream of mushroom soup belongs to British autumn cooking, made when chestnut mushrooms are at their best and the weather turns. Made with dried porcini for depth and proper cream for richness, it is far removed from the tinned version most people grew up with. It also makes a good sauce base, stirred unstrained into chicken or pasta.
